title: Find minimum in sorted rotated array
description: Find the minimum element in a sorted rotated array without duplicates

examples.explanation: Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 3. Iterate: i=1: arr[1]=4, 4 < 3? No, min_val=3. i=2: arr[2]=5, 5 < 3? No, min_val=3. i=3: arr[3]=1, 1 < 3? Yes, min_val=1. i=4: arr[4]=2, 2 < 1? No, min_val=1. Return min_val=1.

Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 7. Iterate: i=1: 8 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=2: 9 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=3: 10 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=4: 11 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=5: 12 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=6: 13 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=7: 14 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=8: 15 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=9: 16 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=10: 17 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=11: 18 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=12: 19 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=13: 20 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=14: 1 < 7? Yes, min_val=1. i=15: 2 < 1? No, min_val=1. i=16: 3 < 1? No, min_val=1. i=17: 4 < 1? No, min_val=1. i=18: 5 < 1? No, min_val=1. i=19: 6 < 1? No, min_val=1. Return min_val=1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array linearly to find the minimum element by comparing each element with the current minimum. Use this when the array is small or as a baseline for understanding the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Minimum in Sorted Rotated Array Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete linear scan solution to find minimum element in sorted rotated array without duplicates.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by scanning the entire array once from left to right, comparing each element with the current minimum found so far. This works because in any array (rotated or not), the minimum element must be present somewhere in the array. By checking every element, we guarantee we'll find it. The intuition is simple: if we don't know where the rotation point is, we must examine all positions. The algorithm initializes min_val with the first element, then iterates through the array. At each position i, it compares arr[i] with min_val. If arr[i] < min_val, it updates min_val to arr[i]. After processing all elements, min_val contains the minimum. Time complexity is O(n) since we visit each element once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a constant amount of extra space. This approach connects to the problem by providing a baseline solution that works for any input size, though it's inefficient for large arrays. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling empty arrays (should return None or raise error), 2) Starting min_val with 0 instead of first element (fails for positive arrays), 3) Off-by-one errors in loop bounds. Real code pattern: def find_min(arr): if not arr: return None; min_val = arr[0]; for num in arr[1:]: if num < min_val: min_val = num; return min_val.

examples.trace: Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 3. Iterate: i=1: arr[1]=4, 4 < 3? No, min_val=3. i=2: arr[2]=5, 5 < 3? No, min_val=3. i=3: arr[3]=1, 1 < 3? Yes, min_val=1. i=4: arr[4]=2, 2 < 1? No, min_val=1. Return min_val=1.

Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 7. Iterate: i=1: 8 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=2: 9 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=3: 10 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=4: 11 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=5: 12 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=6: 13 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=7: 14 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=8: 15 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=9: 16 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=10: 17 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=11: 18 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=12: 19 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=13: 20 < 7? No, min_val=7. i=14: 1 < 7? Yes, min_val=1. i=15: 2 < 1? No, min_val=1. i=16: 3 < 1? No, min_val=1. i=17: 4 < 1? No, min_val=1. i=18: 5 < 1? No, min_val=1. i=19: 6 < 1? No, min_val=1. Return min_val=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Scan to Find Minimum in Any Array
ladders.desc: Core scanning function that finds minimum element by comparing each element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the linear scan algorithm that forms the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to find the minimum in any collection, you must examine every element because you cannot know if a smaller element exists without checking. The algorithm works by maintaining a running minimum: start with the first element as the current minimum, then for each subsequent element, compare it with the current minimum. If the new element is smaller, update the minimum. After processing all elements, the running minimum is the global minimum. Step-by-step: 1) Handle edge case of empty array (return None or raise error). 2) Initialize min_val = arr[0]. 3) For i from 1 to n-1: if arr[i] < min_val, set min_val = arr[i]. 4) Return min_val. Time complexity is O(n) as we iterate through n elements once. Space complexity is O(1) using only constant extra variables. This connects to L0 by being the complete implementation of the scanning logic—L0 just wraps this with problem-specific context. Common mistakes: 1) Using <= instead of < (works but unnecessary comparisons), 2) Starting loop at 0 instead of 1 (compares first element with itself), 3) Not handling single-element arrays correctly. Real code pattern: def linear_scan_min(arr): if len(arr) == 0: return None; min_val = arr[0]; for i in range(1, len(arr)): if arr[i] < min_val: min_val = arr[i]; return min_val.

examples.trace: Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 5. i=1: arr[1]=2, 2 < 5? Yes, min_val=2. i=2: arr[2]=8, 8 < 2? No, min_val=2. i=3: arr[3]=1, 1 < 2? Yes, min_val=1. i=4: arr[4]=9, 9 < 1? No, min_val=1. Return min_val=1.

Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 10. i=1: 20 < 10? No, min_val=10. i=2: 30 < 10? No, min_val=10. i=3: 40 < 10? No, min_val=10. i=4: 50 < 10? No, min_val=10. i=5: 60 < 10? No, min_val=10. i=6: 70 < 10? No, min_val=10. i=7: 80 < 10? No, min_val=10. i=8: 90 < 10? No, min_val=10. i=9: 100 < 10? No, min_val=10. Return min_val=10.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Element Comparison and Minimum Update
ladders.desc: The fundamental comparison operation that determines if a new element is smaller than current minimum.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core comparison logic: given a current minimum value and a new element, determine if the new element is smaller and update the minimum accordingly. The intuition is that finding a minimum requires comparing pairs of values—you can't know what's smallest without comparing. The algorithm is simple: at each step, compare the new element (arr[i]) with the current minimum (min_val). If arr[i] < min_val, then arr[i] becomes the new minimum. Otherwise, min_val remains unchanged. This comparison-update pattern is repeated for each element. Step-by-step for one iteration: 1) Have current min_val. 2) Get next element arr[i]. 3) Compare: if arr[i] < min_val, set min_val = arr[i]. 4) Continue to next element. Time complexity per comparison is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the variables. This connects to L1 as the repeated operation inside the loop—L1 applies this logic to each element in sequence. Common mistakes: 1) Using > instead of < (finds maximum instead), 2) Forgetting to update min_val (just compares without storing result), 3) Comparing wrong indices. Real code pattern: current_min = 5; new_element = 3; if new_element < current_min: current_min = new_element.

examples.trace: Start: current_min = 7, new_element = 4. Compare: 4 < 7? Yes. Update: current_min = 4. Result: updated_min = 4.
Start: current_min = 2, new_element = 5. Compare: 5 < 2? No. Update: current_min remains 2. Result: updated_min = 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array Elements
ladders.desc: Looping through an array to access each element sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental skill of iterating through all elements of an array, which is necessary to apply the comparison logic to each element. The intuition is that to process a collection, you need a mechanism to visit each item one by one. The algorithm uses a loop (typically a for-loop) that goes from the first index to the last. For an array arr of length n, indices range from 0 to n-1. The loop visits each index i, allowing access to arr[i]. Step-by-step: 1) Determine array length n = len(arr). 2) For i in range(n): access arr[i]. 3) Perform operation on arr[i] (e.g., compare). Time complexity for iterating through n elements is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) if not storing all elements. This connects to L2 by providing the framework to apply the comparison to each element—L2's logic happens inside this loop. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors (using i <= n instead of i < n), 2) Modifying array while iterating (can cause errors), 3) Using wrong loop type (while vs for). Real code pattern: arr = [3, 1, 4]; for i in range(len(arr)): element = arr[i]; print(element) outputs 3, 1, 4.

examples.trace: Array length n = 3. Loop: i=0: access arr[0]=5. i=1: access arr[1]=2. i=2: access arr[2]=8. End loop. Elements accessed: 5, 2, 8.
Array length n = 4. Loop: i=0: access arr[0]=10. i=1: access arr[1]=20. i=2: access arr[2]=30. i=3: access arr[3]=40. End loop. Elements accessed: 10, 20, 30, 40.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Element by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieving the value at a specific position in an array.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operation of accessing an element in an array using its index. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each position has a numerical index starting from 0. To get an element, you specify the index, and the system returns the value stored at that position. The algorithm is straightforward: given an array arr and an index i, return arr[i]. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. Step-by-step: 1) Have array arr. 2) Choose index i (must be 0 <= i < len(arr)). 3) Use arr[i] to access element. Time complexity is O(1) for direct access. Space complexity is O(1) as no extra space needed. This connects to L3 as the operation performed inside the loop—L3 uses this to get each element during iteration. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (arr[1] for first element instead of arr[0]), 2) Index out of bounds (accessing arr[3] for length 3 array), 3) Confusing index with value. Real code pattern: arr = [5, 6, 7]; element = arr[1]; element is 6.

examples.trace: Array arr has elements: index 0: 3, index 1: 7, index 2: 2. Access arr[1]: return value at index 1, which is 7.
Array arr has elements: index 0: 100, index 1: 200, index 2: 300, index 3: 400. Access arr[3]: return value at index 3, which is 400.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic concept of what an array is and how indexing works.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the foundational concept of arrays and indexing for complete beginners. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in a specific order. Each element has a position called an index, which is typically a number starting from 0. For example, an array of numbers [10, 20, 30] has three elements: the first element (10) is at index 0, the second (20) at index 1, and the third (30) at index 2. The intuition is that arrays allow you to group related data together and access individual items by their position. Key points: 1) Arrays are ordered (elements have a fixed sequence). 2) Indices are zero-based (first element is index 0). 3) You can access elements using square brackets: arr[index]. 4) The length of an array is the number of elements it contains. Step-by-step to understand: Imagine a row of boxes numbered 0, 1, 2,... Each box holds a value. To get a value, you look in the box with that number. Time and space analysis isn't applicable here as it's a conceptual foundation. This connects to L4 as the prerequisite knowledge—L4 assumes you know what an array and index are. Common mistakes: 1) Thinking index starts at 1, 2) Confusing index with value, 3) Not understanding that arrays can hold any data type. Real code pattern: arr = [5, 10, 15] creates an array; arr[0] gives 5.

examples.trace: The array has three elements. Position 0 (first) holds 'apple'. Position 1 (second) holds 'banana'. Position 2 (third) holds 'cherry'. So indices map to values: index 0 → apple, index 1 → banana, index 2 → cherry.
The array has two elements. The first element (index 0) is 100. The second element (index 1) is 200. So to access 200, use index 1.

approaches.name: Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Use binary search on the rotated array by comparing the middle element with the rightmost element to determine which half contains the minimum. This approach exploits the sorted rotated structure for efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Minimum in Sorted Rotated Array
ladders.desc: Complete binary search solution to find minimum element in sorted rotated array without duplicates
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum element in a sorted array that has been rotated an unknown number of times. The key insight is that even though the array is rotated, it still maintains partial sorted properties that binary search can exploit. The algorithm works by comparing the middle element with the rightmost element at each step. If nums[mid] > nums[right], the minimum must be in the right half because the rotation point (where the sorted order breaks) is there. If nums[mid] <= nums[right], the minimum is in the left half or could be the middle element itself. We continue narrowing the search space until left and right converge. The intuition: In a rotated sorted array, the minimum element is the only element where the next element is smaller (except at boundaries), and it's always at the rotation point. By comparing with the rightmost element, we can determine which side of the array is properly sorted versus which contains the rotation discontinuity. Time complexity is O(log n) since we halve the search space each iteration. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use pointers. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete binary search loop with proper termination conditions. Common mistakes include: 1) Using leftmost element instead of rightmost for comparison (less reliable), 2) Not handling the <= condition correctly, 3) Off-by-one errors in pointer updates. Real code pattern: Initialize left=0, right=len(nums)-1. While left < right: mid = (left+right)//2. If nums[mid] > nums[right]: left = mid+1 else: right = mid. Return nums[left].

examples.trace:
Step 1: left=0, right=6, nums=[4,5,6,7,0,1,2]
Step 2: mid=3, nums[mid]=7, nums[right]=2, 7>2 → left=mid+1=4
Step 3: left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[mid]=1, nums[right]=2, 1<=2 → right=mid=5
Step 4: left=4, right=5, mid=4, nums[mid]=0, nums[right]=1, 0<=1 → right=mid=4
Step 5: left=4, right=4 → loop ends, return nums[4]=0

Step 1: left=0, right=4, nums=[3,4,5,1,2]
Step 2: mid=2, nums[mid]=5, nums[right]=2, 5>2 → left=mid+1=3
Step 3: left=3, right=4, mid=3, nums[mid]=1, nums[right]=2, 1<=2 → right=mid=3
Step 4: left=3, right=3 → loop ends, return nums[3]=1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search with Right Comparison
ladders.desc: Binary search implementation that compares middle element with right boundary
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core binary search logic that compares the middle element with the rightmost element to determine search direction. The intuition: In a rotated sorted array, comparing with the right boundary tells us which side is properly sorted. If nums[mid] > nums[right], the right side must contain smaller elements (including the minimum) because the rotation point is there. If nums[mid] <= nums[right], the right side is properly sorted, so the minimum must be at mid or to the left. The algorithm: 1) Calculate mid index, 2) Compare nums[mid] with nums[right], 3) Update left or right pointer accordingly. Time complexity is O(log n) per search, space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the repeated operation in the full solution. Common mistakes: 1) Using > instead of >= (but with no duplicates, > works), 2) Updating pointers incorrectly. Real code pattern: while left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] > nums[right]: left = mid+1 else: right = mid.

examples.trace: Step 1: mid=3, nums[mid]=7, nums[right]=2, 7>2 → left=mid+1=4, right stays 6
Step 1: mid=2, nums[mid]=5, nums[right]=2, 5>2 → left=mid+1=3, right stays 4

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compare Middle with Right Boundary
ladders.desc: The comparison logic that determines search direction in rotated array
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: comparing the middle element with the right boundary reveals which half contains the minimum. The intuition: In a rotated sorted array without duplicates, exactly one of the two halves (left or right of mid) will be properly sorted. If nums[mid] > nums[right], the right half cannot be sorted (because in a sorted array, elements increase left to right), so the rotation point and minimum must be in the right half. If nums[mid] <= nums[right], the right half is sorted (all elements from mid to right are in order), so the minimum must be at mid or in the left half. This comparison works because the rotation creates a discontinuity where a larger element appears before a smaller one. By checking against the right boundary, we identify which side has this discontinuity. Time/space: O(1) for the comparison. This feeds into L1 as the decision-making step in binary search. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing the comparison direction, 2) Not understanding why right boundary works better than left. Real code pattern: if nums[mid] > nums[right]: search_right() else: search_left().

examples.trace: nums[mid]=7, nums[right]=2, 7>2 → right half contains smaller elements, so search right
nums[mid]=5, nums[right]=2, 5>2 → right half contains smaller elements, so search right

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Standard binary search to find target in sorted array
ladders.explanation: Binary search is the fundamental algorithm that enables O(log n) search by repeatedly halving the search space. The intuition: In a sorted array, comparing the middle element with the target tells us whether to search left or right. If target equals middle, found. If target < middle, search left half (because array is sorted ascending). If target > middle, search right half. The algorithm: 1) Initialize left=0, right=n-1, 2) While left <= right: calculate mid, compare with target, update pointers, 3) Return result. Time complexity is O(log n), space is O(1). This is a prerequisite for L2 because rotated array binary search adapts this pattern. The connection: Instead of comparing with a fixed target, we compare with boundary elements to find the rotation point. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one in termination condition, 2) Integer overflow in mid calculation (use left+(right-left)//2). Real code pattern: while left <= right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] == target: return mid; elif nums[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=4, mid=2, nums[2]=5 == target → return 2

Step 1: left=0, right=4, mid=2, nums[2]=6 < 7 → left=3
Step 2: left=3, right=4, mid=3, nums[3]=8 > 7 → right=2
Step 3: left=3, right=2 → loop ends, return -1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare two array elements and make decision based on result
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing elements at different indices in an array and using the result to guide further computation. The intuition: Comparisons (like >, <, ==) between elements reveal relationships that algorithms can exploit. In binary search, we compare the middle element with a target or boundary to decide search direction. The operation: access array elements using indices, apply comparison operator, use boolean result in control flow. Time/space: O(1). This feeds into L3 as the core operation in binary search decisions. Common mistakes: 1) Comparing indices instead of values, 2) Using wrong comparison operator. Real code pattern: if nums[i] > nums[j]: do_something() else: do_something_else().

examples.trace: nums[3]=7, nums[6]=2, 7 > 2 → True
nums[2]=3, nums[4]=5, 3 > 5 → False

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite: understanding how to access elements in an array using their positions (indices). An array is a contiguous block of elements where each element has an index starting from 0. The intuition: Arrays allow random access - we can directly access any element if we know its index in constant time. The operation: array_name[index] returns the value at that position. Time complexity: O(1). Space: O(1). This feeds into L4 as the way to get elements for comparison. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (arrays are 0-based), 2) Accessing out of bounds indices. Real code pattern: element = nums[i] to get value at index i.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0:4, 1:5, 2:6, 3:7, 4:0, 5:1, 6:2. nums[3] returns element at position 3, which is 7.
Array indices: 0:1, 1:2, 2:3, 3:4, 4:5. nums[0] returns first element, which is 1.

approaches.name: Recursive Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Apply binary search recursively by checking if the middle element is greater than the right element to decide which subarray to search next. This method is similar to iterative binary search but uses recursion for clarity.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(log n) due to recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Minimum in Sorted Rotated Array Using Recursive Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find minimum element in sorted rotated array using recursive binary search.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum element in a sorted array that has been rotated an unknown number of times. The key insight is that even after rotation, the array maintains a partially sorted property that allows binary search adaptation. The recursive binary search approach works by comparing the middle element with the rightmost element at each step. If nums[mid] > nums[right], the minimum must be in the right half (mid+1 to right) because the rotation point lies there. Otherwise, the minimum is in the left half (left to mid). This reduces the search space by half each time, achieving O(log n) time complexity. The recursion continues until left == right, at which point we've found the minimum. The space complexity is O(log n) due to the recursion stack depth. This approach is elegant because it directly targets the rotation point without needing to find it explicitly. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison conditions (using >= instead of > when no duplicates exist), forgetting base cases, and mishandling array bounds. The recursive implementation makes the divide-and-conquer logic clear but requires understanding recursion termination conditions.

examples.trace:
Initial call: findMin([4,5,6,7,0,1,2], 0, 6)
left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[mid]=7, nums[right]=2
Since 7 > 2, search right half: findMin([4,5,6,7,0,1,2], 4, 6)
left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[mid]=1, nums[right]=2
Since 1 <= 2, search left half: findMin([4,5,6,7,0,1,2], 4, 5)
left=4, right=5, mid=4, nums[mid]=0, nums[right]=1
Since 0 <= 1, search left half: findMin([4,5,6,7,0,1,2], 4, 4)
Base case reached: left == right = 4, return nums[4] = 0

Initial call: findMin([3,4,5,1,2], 0, 4)
left=0, right=4, mid=2, nums[mid]=5, nums[right]=2
Since 5 > 2, search right half: findMin([3,4,5,1,2], 3, 4)
left=3, right=4, mid=3, nums[mid]=1, nums[right]=2
Since 1 <= 2, search left half: findMin([3,4,5,1,2], 3, 3)
Base case reached: left == right = 3, return nums[3] = 1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Binary Search on Rotated Array
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive binary search function that finds minimum in rotated sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that implements the binary search logic for rotated sorted arrays. The function takes an array and left/right indices as parameters. At each recursive call, it calculates the mid index. The key decision logic compares nums[mid] with nums[right]. If nums[mid] > nums[right], the rotation point (and thus minimum) must be in the right half because the right half contains elements that wrap around from the end to the beginning of the original sorted array. Therefore, we recursively search indices mid+1 to right. Otherwise, the minimum is in the left half (including mid), so we search left to mid. The base case occurs when left == right, meaning we've narrowed down to a single element which must be the minimum. The time complexity is O(log n) as we halve the search space each time. Space complexity is O(log n) for the recursion stack. This sub-routine directly implements the divide-and-conquer strategy without worrying about input validation or wrapper functions. Common mistakes include using >= instead of > (unnecessary with no duplicates), incorrect recursive bounds (off-by-one errors), and missing base cases. The elegance lies in how a simple comparison tells us which half contains the rotation point.

examples.trace:
Call 1: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[mid]=7, nums[right]=2, 7>2 → search right (4,6)
Call 2: left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[mid]=1, nums[right]=2, 1<=2 → search left (4,5)
Call 3: left=4, right=5, mid=4, nums[mid]=0, nums[right]=1, 0<=1 → search left (4,4)
Call 4: left=4, right=4 → base case, return nums[4]=0

Call 1: left=0, right=4, mid=2, nums[mid]=3, nums[right]=5, 3<=5 → search left (0,2)
Call 2: left=0, right=2, mid=1, nums[mid]=2, nums[right]=3, 2<=3 → search left (0,1)
Call 3: left=0, right=1, mid=0, nums[mid]=1, nums[right]=2, 1<=2 → search left (0,0)
Call 4: left=0, right=0 → base case, return nums[0]=1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compare Middle with Right Element to Determine Search Direction
ladders.desc: Understand how comparing middle element with right element tells which half contains minimum.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of the algorithm. In a rotated sorted array without duplicates, comparing the middle element (nums[mid]) with the rightmost element (nums[right]) of the current search range provides crucial information about where the minimum element lies. If nums[mid] > nums[right], it means the middle element is larger than the rightmost element. In a normally sorted array, this would be impossible (since sorted order means elements increase left to right). The only way this can happen is if the rotation point (where the array wraps) lies between mid and right. Therefore, the minimum must be in the right half (from mid+1 to right). Conversely, if nums[mid] <= nums[right], the segment from mid to right is properly sorted (non-decreasing), so the minimum cannot be after mid—it must be in the left half (from left to mid). This single comparison effectively tells us which half to discard, similar to standard binary search but adapted for rotation. The intuition comes from recognizing that rotation creates two sorted segments, and comparing with the right endpoint helps identify which segment we're in. This logic works because there are no duplicates; with duplicates, we'd need more careful handling. Understanding this comparison is key to adapting binary search for rotated arrays.

examples.trace:
nums[mid]=7, nums[right]=2
7 > 2 is true
This indicates rotation point is in right half
Therefore minimum is in indices 4-6

nums[mid]=5, nums[right]=2
5 > 2 is true
Rotation point is between indices 2 and 4
Therefore search indices 3-4

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Implement standard binary search to find target in sorted array.
ladders.explanation: Binary search is a fundamental algorithm that efficiently finds a target value in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. The intuition is that because the array is sorted, comparing the target with the middle element tells us whether the target could be in the left half or right half, allowing us to discard half of the remaining elements each step. The algorithm maintains two pointers, left and right, representing the current search range. At each step, calculate mid = (left + right) // 2. If nums[mid] equals the target, return mid. If target < nums[mid], search the left half by setting right = mid - 1. If target > nums[mid], search the right half by setting left = mid + 1. Continue until left > right, indicating the target is not found. Time complexity is O(log n) as the search space halves each iteration. Space complexity is O(1) for iterative implementation or O(log n) for recursive. This building block is essential because the rotated array minimum problem adapts this same divide-and-conquer approach, but with a different comparison logic. Understanding standard binary search helps recognize how to modify it for rotated arrays. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in calculating mid or updating bounds, and infinite recursion if base cases are wrong.

examples.trace: left=0, right=4, mid=2, nums[mid]=5 → found at index 2

left=0, right=4, mid=2, nums[mid]=6, 7>6 → left=3
left=3, right=4, mid=3, nums[mid]=8, 7<8 → right=2
left=3, right=2 → stop, not found

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Elements and Calculate Mid Index
ladders.desc: Perform element comparison and compute middle index in array search.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on two fundamental operations used in binary search algorithms: comparing array elements and calculating the middle index. Comparing elements (e.g., nums[mid] > nums[right]) is crucial for making decisions about which part of the array to search next. In programming, this is typically done using relational operators (<, >, <=, >=). Calculating the middle index correctly is essential to avoid infinite loops or incorrect results. The standard formula is mid = (left + right) // 2 using integer division to get the floor value. This ensures mid is an integer index within the bounds. An alternative to prevent potential overflow in some languages is mid = left + (right - left) // 2. These operations are simple but must be done precisely. The comparison tells us about the relative ordering of elements, while the mid calculation ensures we split the search range properly. Together, they form the repetitive steps in binary search. Understanding these operations helps build intuition for how algorithms examine data and make decisions. Common mistakes include using floating-point division instead of integer division, or incorrect comparison operators (e.g., using == when <= is needed).

examples.trace:
Calculate mid: (0+6)//2 = 3
Access nums[3] = 7
Access nums[6] = 2
Compare: 7 > 2 is true

Calculate mid: (4+6)//2 = 5
Access nums[5] = 1
Access nums[6] = 2
Compare: 1 > 2 is false

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access array elements using indices and understand zero-based indexing.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept required for array algorithms. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element has an index (position) starting from 0 in most programming languages (zero-based indexing). To access an element, we use the syntax array[index], which retrieves the value at that position. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. Indices must be within the valid range [0, length-1] to avoid errors. Understanding this allows us to traverse arrays, compare elements, and perform searches. In binary search, we constantly access elements at specific indices (like mid, left, right) to make decisions. This foundation is crucial because all array algorithms build upon accessing and manipulating elements via indices. Beginners should practice accessing elements, understanding that indices are integers, and that going out of bounds causes errors. This concept connects to higher levels where we use indices to implement search algorithms.

examples.trace:
Array has 7 elements indexed 0 to 6
Index 3 refers to the fourth element
Count: index0=4, index1=5, index2=6, index3=7
So array[3] = 7

Array has 5 elements indexed 0 to 4
Index 4 refers to the fifth element
Count: index0=3, index1=4, index2=5, index3=1, index4=2
So array[4] = 2
